speaker: interviewer
text: I have just laid it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Off the record.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: A certain amount of philosophical [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] thinking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] has to do with post-trauma, like that, and also psychological. And I-- I would imagine that my personality, as such, would be different, had I not experienced-- or my behavior patterns, or my selecting a wife, or whatever, all of that is interconnected.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can go ahead and slate, if you want. And then just go right on.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five, with survivor Jerry Gerard. Interviewer, Miriam Coral. Los Angeles, California. March 31, 1995. Tape five. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How-- how has your war experience affected you? How has it had an effect on who you are? Do you-- does it linger with you all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I don't think it lingers with me all the time but it is, obviously, a part of me, since-- the fortune-- the fortunate thing, I think, in my case, was that I had, really, a wonderful childhood up until age 13. That gave me a psychological foundation. And also, maybe, my being-- or whatever it is that makes me me-- combined, gave me the psychological substance to cope with the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] terrible conditions. Terrible is not the word. I'm-- I'm missing the word, but the inhumane and the genocide, period, being a witness to it, it made me. It helped me to cope. That is still a part of me, in that, as the war ended, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the four or five months I spent with the American company, and then the subsequent re-acquaintance with family members and making friends, including finding those friends that I had known before, gave me a new-- a new impetus, and I started making myself independent again, without fail. I worked very hard, from my arrival here, '47 until, let's say, 10 years back, to establish an independence that I was able to achieve without any outside help. And I count my blessings that I am blessed with, certainly, means that make it possible to lead a life that lacks any kind of deprivation, that has given me the pleasure of boating, and the pleasure of raising my children, and giving them a basic background that I am proud of, of giving them each a home that they are either living in, or were able to sell and develop into more investment. And so I am also, therefore, charitable. Not because of that only, but I am living in a charitable lifestyle, and we are very active in United Jewish Fund. We're active in Beit T'Shuvah, which Betty has been a president of-- president of in the-- in the friends' group. And we're active in the Anti-Defamation League, and Cedars-Sinai Medallion Group, and the Vista del Mar. And we have recently formed the OSE USA Group, which is that same Jewish organization in France, that received us when we arrived from Germany. We now have formed an American [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] affiliate organization, so that we can raise funds here, in this country, to stipulate where, specifically, these funds can be used in the French organization. So that I am blessed with, not only having survived, but with playing a part in-- in-- fortunate part of being charitable. That is tzedakah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You said, earlier, that you had a strong faith in God, and that helped you through. Is that still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A faith in God is what I am certain helped me realize that the incredible inhumanities that I was witnessing could not last, that it was just a matter of time, that sooner or later, it would have to end. There were no other enforcements given to me, to believe that. It was just this other force, which you can call God, or you can call a power that is out of our control, above and beyond us, that I call God. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then and I, you know, we're active in the Valley Beth Shalom, our temple. And I'm very fond of Rabbi Harold Schulweis, who is our rabbi-- who also married us, right here, where we sit. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I am an active Jew. I am not observing, as my father was. I am not keeping a kosher household. But I am a Jew, and I'm proud of it. And I'm tolerant of all those who pursue their lifestyle, as long as it's within-- within respect to another human being. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are now going to look at your pictures. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of my maternal grandparents, Ferdinand Stern and [INAUDIBLE] Stern. It was taken near the city of Hanover, in 1900-- two years before my mother was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hanover, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. My grandfather was a banker. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of my father and mother, taken, I imagine, around 1920, at the time they were married. His name is Josef Rosenzweig, and her name, Gertrude Rosenzweig. She was 18 years old, at the time. My father was, I think, 29 at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Bunde, Westfalen, which is also near Hanover, in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of me, Gerhard Rosenzweig, at the age of about, maybe, a year and few months, in 1926. I was born in '25. And it's in Bunde again, Bunde, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did these pictures survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's from my aunt. If you look in the back, it's torn from an album. Are we still on? Oh, this was given to me by either my sister or several of my family members. I ended up with nothing personal when I came out of camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the pictures of your parents, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. All of them are all from other--

speaker: interviewer
text: From your aunt or your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: From my aunt or from-- from a cousin, or my sister, or other. We have a very large family, many of whom are not living, anymore. But we did have a large family. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a group picture of my father, his mother, my grandmother-- paternal grandmother-- his sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you identify each one?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his niece. Oops. OK, the niece is the one with the dog. Her name is Elsa [? Sacks. ?] She is living in North Vancouver. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everyone else in that picture, except me, are no longer living. The lady behind her, who was my father's sister, Bella, whom I also was very close to, and in whose house I spent almost every summer for about three or four years in a row.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Bella Isaac. And her husband is sitting in the foreground. He was a rabbi, like my dad. His name was [? Isidor ?] Isaac. What's amazing about him and his wife and the daughter, for that matter, they all moved to Israel in about 1936, and established a farm. And then my grandmother, my maternal grandmother, is to my right, to the left of me in the picture.

words.text: to the left of me in the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's the one in the center?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the center, with the white hair. And her name was Rosa. And I would say, at that time, she was about 75 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Full name was Rosa Rosenzweig. And behind me is my father, in a hat. His face is partially in shadow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And his name is Josef Rosenzweig. I'm in front of him. I was nine years old. So it was taken at about--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jerry, or Gerhard Rosenzweig. And the picture was taken in 1934. My age was nine, nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Limburg, L-I-M-B-U-R-G. Limburg is a town near Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. This is the house I grew up in, in Brandenburg, Germany. It's called a gemeinde house, which means a congregational house. The living quarters were upstairs, where we lived. And there was also another upstairs, which doesn't show. It a mansard with the windows facing the other way. The lower part of the house is the room and the quarters where religious school took place, and other communal activities. The synagogue is behind the house and to the right is a public school. You had to-- you had to go through our house to get to the synagogue, through that portal that you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when was this picture taken, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was probably taken before we lived there, it looks like, to me. I would say it was taken in 1925. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a partial view of the interior of the synagogue in Brandenburg. The Synagogue of Brandenburg [GERMAN]. It does not show the-- the back. The picture was not taken at the back of the synagogue, so it was larger. This was destroyed in-- on November 9, 1938-- burnt, and totally destroyed. Does not exist. I, Gerhard Rosenzweig, was bar mitzvahed there, in 1938, in September-- so about, maybe, six weeks before it was destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was the rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a rabbi in this synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken again?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken, probably, in the '30s. I don't know, exactly-- in the mid-'30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Brandenburg, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brandenburg, Germany. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of my late father, Rabbinan Cantor Josef Rosenzweig. This was taken, I would guess, in 1940. So he was about 50 years old, at the time. He had already left Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in France. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture of me, Gerhard Rosenzweig-- Jerry Gerard now-- at age 13. It was taken in June of 1939. I sent this to my sister for her birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Berlin,

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you, at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at the [INAUDIBLE] at the time. This was about a month before I came to France. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Many pictures of me, at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is a picture of the group that was together in the center of central France. And it was probably taken in 1941. I am on the lower right. And the people taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am is--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Jerry Gerard, or Gerhard Rosenzweig. I am on the lower right. In the center row, second from the left, and in the top row, last on the left, and the bottom row last on the left, are three of the six boys that ended up in concentration camp, from this home, where the picture was taken, the following year. The only survivor, besides myself, is in the center row, second from the left. He lives in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolfgang Blumenreich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken, exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken at Chabannes, near Limoges in France, in-- I would say 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the OSE?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the OSE home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you, in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, I was 15 or 16, 15. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is Chateau de Chabannes, which is a large residence, where our home was in central France. And this shot was probably taken in 1942. This home housed approximately 150 children. And it was coed. And there are some annexes that are not shown on this house. And it was the one thing that many of us cherished-- the seat of our common experiences, during the war and before the war. And as a matter of fact, we revisited this home in 1988, when we had a reunion. It is unoccupied today. It had been on the market. It is owned by someone. And it is the seat of a very strong bond between those of us who are still in touch with one another. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whoops. Just as we start, it starts to fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you shut off the camera?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at all. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself with a unknown young German lady, taken soon after my liberation-- I would say in-- probably in June or July of 1945. I was in American uniform. I was with the Army, at the time, and I had already overcome my undernourished condition. Doesn't take long. It was about two months after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you identify yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Gerhard Rosenzweig, or Jerry Gerard, today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- I can't tell you, exactly-- probably in Munich. And it was sometime in 1945-- I would say in July, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Munich, Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of myself, Gerhard Rosenzweig, and my mother, Gertrude Rosenzweig, taken in Hyde Park, London in, my guess is, February of 1946, the first time I went to visit her, after the end of the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in England-- Hyde Park, London, England. It's where she lived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a group shot of, you might call it an alma mater, or of a group of us that had a reunion near Paris, in 1988, near Paris, France. Myself, Gerhard Rosenzweig, or Jerry Gerard, I am in the center of the picture, lower row with a red tie. And above me, next to the lady in pink, with a gray suit is Wolfgang Blumenreich, the other-- only other survivor of the camp. And we are in touch with all these-- with one another. All these people that you see on this picture are in touch with one another, to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these people are from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're from various parts of the world. They are Canadians, Israeli, Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, what makes them a group?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fact that we were all together in a children's home in central France, from 1940 until-- some of them, later. But for myself, until '42 . Some of them left in '41, some of them were there until the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all Jewish refugees. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture shows Betty's and my family. I am Jerry Gerard, born Gerhard Rosenzweig, on the far right in the brown shirt. Betty is on the far left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Betty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betty Gerard my wife. And it shows, starting on my right, Bob Gerard, my oldest son, his brother, Steve Gerard, with his wife, Trudy, in red, and Michael Spiegel, Betty's oldest son, and David Spiegel, Betty's younger son. This picture was taken, I would say, in 1984. So it's about nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in our home, right here in Sherman Oaks, Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of our grandchildren, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Julie Gerard, who is the girl in the picture, dancing. She's going on six years old. And Benjamin, who is three years old. Taken in San Francisco at our son's house, Dr. Steve Gerard. And I am Gerhard Rosenzweig, now Jerry Gerard, talking. This picture was taken in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the beginning of March of 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we are looking at a picture of Hannelore Gable, my sister, my late sister. This was taken in 1977, at the time in Las Vegas. She passed away in San Francisco. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Las Vegas, Nevada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Las Vegas, Nevada, and she was, at the time-- let's see, 1922, 1977-- she was 55 years old. Passed away in 1978. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my paternal grandfather, Rabbi [? Isidor ?] Rosenzweig. It was taken in Marburg, Germany-- a city not far from Frankfurt. My estimated time of when it was taken was 1920, before I was born. He died in 1924, a year before I was born. He was a rabbi in a small town called [? Gladenbach ?].
